concepts.title: Chemical Reactions and Chemical Equations
concepts.definition: A chemical reaction is a process in which one or more substances (reactants) change to form new substances (products) with different properties. A chemical equation is a symbolic representation of this reaction using chemical formulae.
examples: Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium oxide (word equation).
2Mg + O2 → 2MgO (balanced chemical equation).

concepts.title: Balancing Chemical Equations
concepts.definition: Chemical equations are balanced so that the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides, satisfying the law of conservation of mass.
examples: Unbalanced: Fe + O2 → Fe2O3; Balanced: 4Fe + 3O2 → 2Fe2O3.

concepts.title: Types of Chemical Reactions
concepts.definition: Common types include combination, decomposition, displacement, double displacement and redox reactions, based on how reactants change to products.
examples: Combination: 2H2 + O2 → 2H2O.
Decomposition: 2KClO3 → 2KCl + 3O2 (on heating).
Displacement: Zn + CuSO4 → ZnSO4 + Cu.
Double displacement: Na2SO4 + BaCl2 → BaSO4 + 2NaCl.

concepts.title: Effects of Oxidation and Reduction: Corrosion and Rancidity
concepts.definition: Oxidation is gain of oxygen or loss of electrons; reduction is loss of oxygen or gain of electrons. Slow oxidation can cause corrosion of metals and rancidity of fats and oils.
examples: Rusting of iron: 4Fe + 3O2 + 2xH2O → 2Fe2O3·xH2O.
Chips packed in nitrogen to slow rancidity of oil.

concepts.title: Acids and Bases and Their Indicators
concepts.definition: Acids produce H+ ions in aqueous solution; bases produce OH− ions. Indicators like litmus, phenolphthalein and methyl orange show different colours in acids and bases.
examples: HCl is an acid, NaOH is a base.
Blue litmus turns red in acids; red litmus turns blue in bases.

concepts.title: Chemical Properties of Acids and Bases
concepts.definition: Acids and bases show characteristic reactions with metals, metal oxides, carbonates and each other, often producing salts and other compounds.
examples: Acid + metal → salt + H2 (e.g., 2HCl + Zn → ZnCl2 + H2).
Base + acid → salt + water (neutralisation): NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H2O.

concepts.title: pH and Its Importance
concepts.definition: pH is a measure of acidity or basicity of a solution on a scale from 0 to 14, defined as negative logarithm of hydrogen ion concentration.
examples: pH < 7 indicates acidic, pH = 7 neutral, pH > 7 basic.
Human blood has pH around 7.4.

concepts.title: Salts and Their Uses
concepts.definition: Salts are ionic compounds formed by neutralisation of acids and bases and have many household and industrial uses.
examples: Common salt NaCl, baking soda NaHCO3, washing soda Na2CO3·10H2O, plaster of Paris CaSO4·½H2O.

concepts.title: Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals
concepts.definition: Metals are generally lustrous, malleable, ductile, good conductors and sonorous; non‑metals are usually dull, brittle in solid state and poor conductors.
examples: Iron, copper and aluminium are metals; sulphur and phosphorus are non‑metals.

concepts.title: Chemical Properties of Metals
concepts.definition: Metals react with oxygen, water, acids and other salts; reactivity varies and is summarised in the reactivity series.
examples: 4Na + O2 → 2Na2O; 2Mg + O2 → 2MgO.
Metal + acid → salt + H2, e.g., Zn + H2SO4 → ZnSO4 + H2.

concepts.title: Ionic Compounds
concepts.definition: Ionic compounds form due to transfer of electrons from metals to non‑metals, producing oppositely charged ions held by electrostatic forces.
examples: Formation of NaCl from Na and Cl; CaCl2 from Ca and Cl2.

concepts.title: Corrosion and Its Prevention
concepts.definition: Corrosion is the gradual damage of metals by chemical reactions with environment, especially air and moisture. It can be reduced by protective coatings or sacrificial metals.
examples: Galvanisation of iron by zinc coating; painting and oiling metal surfaces.

concepts.title: Versatile Nature of Carbon
concepts.definition: Carbon forms a vast number of compounds due to catenation (self‑linking) and tetravalency, allowing chains, branches and rings.
examples: Chains in alkanes like hexane; rings in benzene; branched isomers like isobutane.

concepts.title: Covalent Bonding in Carbon Compounds
concepts.definition: Carbon shares electrons with other atoms to form covalent bonds, resulting in stable molecules of organics compounds.
examples: Methane CH4, ethene C2H4, carbon dioxide CO2.

concepts.title: Homologous Series and Functional Groups
concepts.definition: Homologous series is a family of organic compounds with same functional group and general formula, differing by CH2 unit. Functional groups decide chemical properties.
examples: Alkane series: CnH2n+2; alcohol group –OH; carboxylic acid group –COOH.

concepts.title: Important Carbon Compounds: Ethanol and Ethanoic Acid
concepts.definition: Ethanol and ethanoic acid are common organic compounds with wide use as solvent, fuel and acid, showing typical reactions of alcohols and carboxylic acids.
examples: Ethanol C2H5OH used in spirit; ethanoic acid CH3COOH in vinegar.
Esterification: alcohol + acid → ester + water.

concepts.title: Soaps and Detergents (Intro)
concepts.definition: Soaps and detergents are cleansing agents that help remove dirt and oil by forming micelles, but differ in composition and action in hard water.
examples: Soap is sodium or potassium salt of fatty acid; detergents are sulphonic acid salts effective in hard water.

concepts.title: Need for Classification and Early Attempts
concepts.definition: As number of known elements increased, classification was needed to organise them based on properties; attempts include Dobereiner’s triads and Newlands’ law of octaves.
examples: Dobereiner grouped Li, Na, K as a triad; Newlands arranged elements so every eighth had similar properties.

concepts.title: Modern Periodic Table
concepts.definition: Modern periodic table arranges elements in increasing atomic number, in rows (periods) and columns (groups) so that elements with similar valence shell configuration fall in same group.
examples: Group 1 alkali metals: Li, Na, K; Group 17 halogens: F, Cl, Br, I.

concepts.title: Trends in the Periodic Table
concepts.definition: Properties such as atomic size, metallic character, valency and electronegativity show regular trends along periods and groups.
examples: Atomic radius decreases across a period, increases down a group; metallic character decreases across a period, increases down a group.

concepts.title: Life Processes and Their Types
concepts.definition: Life processes are activities essential for maintaining life, including nutrition, respiration, transportation and excretion.
examples: Plants performing photosynthesis, animals respiring for energy, circulation of blood in humans.

concepts.title: Nutrition in Plants and Animals
concepts.definition: Nutrition is intake and use of food materials. Plants are autotrophs making food by photosynthesis; most animals are heterotrophs depending on others.
formulae: Photosynthesis: 6CO2 + 6H2O → C6H12O6 + 6O2 (in presence of light and chlorophyll)
examples: Human digestive system with mouth, stomach and intestines; modes like holozoic nutrition.

concepts.title: Respiration: Aerobic and Anaerobic
concepts.definition: Respiration is oxidation of food to release energy. Aerobic respiration uses oxygen, while anaerobic does not and yields less energy.
examples: In humans: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water + energy.
Yeast performing anaerobic respiration producing alcohol and CO2.

concepts.title: Transportation in Plants and Animals
concepts.definition: Transport systems move materials like water, minerals, food and gases in organisms. Plants use xylem and phloem; animals use circulatory system with blood and vessels.
examples: Heart pumping blood through arteries and veins; transpiration pull in xylem.

concepts.title: Excretion in Plants and Animals
concepts.definition: Excretion removes metabolic wastes from the body to maintain internal balance. Animals have specialised organs; plants use diffusion and storage.
examples: Human excretory system with kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder and urethra; plants excreting through leaves and gums.

concepts.title: Control and Coordination in Animals
concepts.definition: Coordination is integration of different organs using nervous system and endocrine (hormonal) system.
examples: Brain, spinal cord and nerves transmitting impulses; hormones like insulin regulating blood sugar.

concepts.title: Structure and Function of Neuron
concepts.definition: Neurons are specialised nerve cells with cell body, dendrites and axon that transmit nerve impulses by electrical and chemical signals.
examples: Reflex actions like knee‑jerk using reflex arc involving receptor, sensory neuron, spinal cord, motor neuron and effector.

concepts.title: Plant Hormones and Tropic Movements
concepts.definition: Plants respond to stimuli through hormones and directional growth movements called tropisms.
examples: Auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins and ethylene; phototropism (bending towards light), geotropism (root growing downward).

concepts.title: Asexual Reproduction
concepts.definition: Asexual reproduction involves a single parent producing genetically identical offspring without formation and fusion of gametes.
examples: Binary fission in Amoeba, budding in yeast, fragmentation in Spirogyra, vegetative propagation in plants.

concepts.title: Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
concepts.definition: Sexual reproduction involves formation of male and female gametes, their fusion and development of seeds and fruits.
examples: Structure of flower: stamen (male), pistil (female); pollination and fertilisation.

concepts.title: Reproduction in Humans
concepts.definition: Human reproduction includes male and female reproductive systems, gamete formation, fertilisation, embryo development and birth.
examples: Testes producing sperms; ovaries producing ova; foetal development in uterus.

concepts.title: Heredity and Inheritance
concepts.definition: Heredity is transmission of traits from parents to offspring through genes. Inheritance patterns follow certain rules studied by Mendel.
examples: Mendel’s experiments on pea plants with tall/short, round/wrinkled seeds.

concepts.title: Traits, Genes and Chromosomes
concepts.definition: Traits are characteristics; genes are units of heredity located on chromosomes made of DNA.
examples: Eye colour, height as traits; alleles as different forms of a gene.

concepts.title: Evolution (Concept Level)
concepts.definition: Evolution is gradual change in heritable characteristics of species over generations due to variation and natural selection.
examples: Fossil records, homologous organs and industrial melanism in moths as evidence of evolution.

concepts.title: Reflection of Light by Spherical Mirrors
concepts.definition: Spherical mirrors (concave and convex) reflect light according to laws of reflection; image formation depends on object position.
examples: Concave mirrors form real or virtual images; convex mirrors always form virtual, diminished images.

concepts.title: Refraction of Light and Refractive Index
concepts.definition: Refraction is bending of light when it passes between media of different optical densities; refractive index measures optical density.
examples: Light bending when entering water from air; apparent shift in position of objects under water.

concepts.title: Refraction by Spherical Lenses
concepts.definition: Convex and concave lenses refract light; image formation patterns depend on object distance.
examples: Convex lens can form real or virtual images; concave lens forms virtual, diminished images.

chapters.title: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

concepts.title: Structure and Functioning of Human Eye
concepts.definition: The eye has parts like cornea, iris, pupil, lens, retina and optic nerve that enable vision by focusing light on retina.
examples: Ciliary muscles adjust lens curvature for near and distant vision (accommodation).

concepts.title: Defects of Vision and Correction
concepts.definition: Common eye defects include myopia, hypermetropia and presbyopia, corrected using suitable lenses.
examples: Myopia (short‑sightedness) corrected by concave lens; hypermetropia by convex lens.

concepts.title: Dispersion and Scattering of Light
concepts.definition: Dispersion is splitting of white light into constituent colours; scattering is deviation of light by particles, explaining phenomena like blue sky and red sunsets.
examples: Rainbow formation by dispersion; blue colour of sky due to scattering of shorter wavelength light.

concepts.title: Electric Current and Potential Difference
concepts.definition: Electric current is rate of flow of charge; potential difference is work done per unit charge to move a charge between two points.
examples: Current in a circuit measured in amperes using ammeter; potential difference measured in volts using voltmeter.

concepts.title: Ohm’s Law and Resistance
concepts.definition: Ohm’s law states that current through a conductor is directly proportional to potential difference across it, at constant temperature.
examples: Plot of V vs I for ohmic conductor is straight line through origin; slope gives resistance.

concepts.title: Resistors in Series and Parallel
concepts.definition: Equivalent resistance of resistors depends on whether they are connected in series or parallel.
examples: Series connection increases total resistance; parallel connection decreases it.

concepts.title: Electric Power and Heating Effect
concepts.definition: Electric power is rate of doing work or energy consumption; current through resistance produces heat (Joule heating).
examples: Electric heater, bulb and fuse working based on heating effect of current.

concepts.title: Magnetic Field and Field Lines
concepts.definition: A current‑carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it; field lines represent direction and strength of field.
examples: Right‑hand thumb rule gives direction of field around straight current‑carrying conductor.

concepts.title: Force on Current-Carrying Conductor
concepts.definition: A conductor carrying current in a magnetic field experiences a force; its direction is given by Fleming’s left‑hand rule.
examples: Working principle of electric motor based on this force.

concepts.title: Electromagnetic Induction (Concept)
concepts.definition: Change in magnetic field linked with a conductor induces an electric current in it; direction is given by Fleming’s right‑hand rule.
examples: Working of generator and some power‑generation devices.

concepts.title: Conventional Energy Sources
concepts.definition: Traditional energy sources include fossil fuels (coal, petroleum), hydroelectric power and biomass, widely used but with limitations.
examples: Thermal power plants using coal; dams generating hydroelectricity.

concepts.title: Non-conventional Energy Sources
concepts.definition: Renewable, alternative energy sources like solar, wind, geothermal and tidal energy are cleaner and more sustainable.
examples: Solar panels converting sunlight to electricity; wind turbines generating power from wind.

concepts.title: Qualities of a Good Fuel
concepts.definition: A good fuel has high calorific value, is readily available, economical, easy to store and transport, and produces minimal pollution.
examples: LPG and CNG are considered good fuels compared to coal and firewood.

concepts.title: Ecosystem and Its Components
concepts.definition: An ecosystem includes all living organisms in an area and their physical environment interacting as a system.
examples: Forest, pond and desert ecosystems with producers, consumers and decomposers.

concepts.title: Food Chains and Food Webs
concepts.definition: Food chains show linear feeding relationships; food webs are interconnected food chains showing complex interactions.
examples: Grass → grasshopper → frog → snake → eagle.
Many food chains interconnected in a forest form a food web.

concepts.title: Ozone Layer and Its Depletion
concepts.definition: The ozone layer protects Earth from harmful UV radiation; certain chemicals like CFCs cause its depletion.
examples: International efforts to reduce CFC use to protect ozone layer.

concepts.title: Need for Resource Management
concepts.definition: Natural resources like forests, water and minerals must be managed sustainably to meet present needs without compromising future generations.
examples: Judicious use of water and forests; avoiding over‑exploitation of minerals.

concepts.title: Management of Forests and Wildlife
concepts.definition: Conservation of biodiversity through protected areas, community participation and laws helps maintain ecological balance.
examples: National parks and wildlife sanctuaries; Chipko movement to protect forests.

concepts.title: Water and Energy Management
concepts.definition: Sustainable water and energy management involve conserving, recycling and choosing cleaner options.
examples: Rainwater harvesting, drip irrigation; promoting renewable energy sources.

concepts.title: Reduce, Reuse and Recycle
concepts.definition: 3R principle focuses on reducing resource use, reusing materials and recycling waste to reduce environmental impact.
examples: Using cloth bags instead of plastic, reusing containers, recycling paper and metals.
